text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
In our last study, we saw that the Kohathites carried six holy items on their shoulders through the wilderness, and that the number six speaks of man in his unfinished, wilderness-walking condition; chosen by God to carry what is perfect, even while he himself is still being formed. Today we begin to look at each of those six items one by one, starting with the first.

The first item on the list is the Ark of the Covenant, and its position at the top of the list is no accident. God is always intentional in the order He gives things. Numbers 4:5 begins the instructions this way: when the camp is ready to move, the very first thing Aaron and his sons must do is go in and take down the inner veil and cover the Ark. Not the table, not the lampstand, not the altars; the Ark first. It was the first to be prepared, the first to be covered, and the first to be lifted onto Kohathite shoulders when Israel broke camp. The order of the items followed the order of their holiness, and the Ark stood at the very top of that order. It was even the reason the Kohathites were numbered before the other Levite clans in Scripture; even before Gershon, who was the eldest son of Levi. The Ark determined the order of everything.

Before we can understand what it means for us to carry the Ark today, we need to understand what the Ark actually was. We cannot carry something we have never properly seen. So let us open the Ark, so to speak, and look carefully at what was inside it. What we find there will take our breath away, because everything inside the Ark points to Jesus Christ with remarkable precision.

The Ark was a chest made of acacia wood, completely overlaid with gold; not just on the outside, but on the inside as well (Exodus 25:11). Acacia wood is one of the hardest and most durable woods found in the desert. It does not rot.
It resists decay even in the harshest conditions. This speaks of the true humanity of Jesus Christ; real flesh, a real human body, a genuine human nature; but without Sin. He was made like us in every way, yet without sin, as Hebrews 4:15 tells us. The wood is not decoration. It is the structure. It is the foundation that holds everything else together.

The gold overlaying that wood speaks of His full and unbroken holiness. Notice that the gold was on the inside as well. This is a detail worth holding. Many things look holy on the outside but are hollow within. The Ark was not like that. What was on the outside was the same as what was on the inside; pure gold throughout. This is Christ. Colossians 2:9 says, “In Him the whole fullness of divine dwells bodily.” Not a surface holiness. Not an outward appearance of God. The fullness of divine living inside a human frame, with nothing hidden and nothing false. Wood and gold together.

Inside the Ark lay the two stone tablets of the Law; written by the finger of God at Sinai (Exodus 31:18). But do you remember what happened to the first set of tablets? Moses came down from the mountain and found Israel worshipping a golden calf. In his grief and anger, he threw the tablets to the ground and broke them (Exodus 32:19). The Law was broken before it ever reached the people properly. God then gave a second set, and it was this second set that was placed inside the Ark and carried through the wilderness. The broken tablets speak loudly. They speak of Israel’s failure, and of every human being’s failure, to keep the Law in their own strength. No man has ever fully kept the Law from the heart. The breaking of the first tablets is the story of all of us.

But then came the one who did not break the Law. Jesus said, “I have not come to abolish the Law but to fulfil it” (Matthew 5:17). And Psalm 40:8, which is the voice of Christ speaking to the Father, says: “I delight to do Your will, O my God; Your Law is within my heart.” The Law was inside the Ark. The Law was inside Christ written in His very heart, not as a burden He reluctantly bore,
but as the expression of who He truly was. He was the Law lived out in human flesh, perfectly, completely, from the inside out. The second tablets, whole and preserved inside the Ark, speak of this: the Law honoured, satisfied, and kept; not for Himself alone, but on behalf of all who had already broken it.

Numbers 17 tells us the story of the rod. There was rebellion in the camp about who God had chosen as His priest. So God gave a clear sign. Each of the twelve tribes was to bring a rod; a staff - and place it before the Ark overnight. The rod that budded in the morning would belong to God’s chosen man. Twelve dead rods were placed before the presence of God in the darkness. In the morning, eleven were exactly as they had been placed; dry, dead, unchanged. But Aaron’s rod had budded, blossomed, and produced ripe almonds overnight (Numbers 17:8). Life had come from a dead thing, in the presence of God, without soil or water or root. God commanded that this rod be placed inside the Ark as a permanent testimony.

This is one of the most vivid pictures of resurrection in all the Old Testament. The rod was cut off from its tree. It had no life source. It was dead. This is exactly the language Isaiah uses of Christ: “He was cut off from the land of the living” (Isaiah 53:8). Christ was cut off, placed in the darkness of the tomb, separated from everything that sustains natural life. And on the third morning, before the presence of the Father, resurrection life broke out from death; not merely a recovery, not a resuscitation, but a bursting into new and indestructible life, as unexpected and as impossible as ripe almonds on a dead stick of wood.
The almond tree is worth noting here. In Israel, the almond is the first tree to blossom after the long winter. Its Hebrew name, shaqed, means to watch or to hasten. The resurrection of Christ is the signal that winter is over; that death does not have the final word, that God was watching, and the moment He had appointed had arrived.

The rod also speaks of the eternal priesthood of Christ. It was placed in the Ark as the permanent sign of God’s chosen priest. Hebrews 7:16 says that Christ became a priest “not on the basis of a legal requirement concerning bodily descent, but by the power of an indestructible life.” His priesthood is not inherited through a family line. It is proven by resurrection. Because He lives forever, He holds His priesthood forever, and Hebrews 7:25 tells us He always lives to make intercession for us. The rod that bloomed in the darkness of the night is still blooming. The priest who rose from the dead is still serving.

Every morning in the wilderness, God fed Israel with manna; a white, fine, bread-like substance that appeared on the ground with the dew (Exodus 16:14). It came faithfully, six days a week, for forty years. It came in exact measure; each person gathered what they needed, no more and no less. But it could not be stored. If anyone kept it overnight, by morning it was rotten and full of worms (Exodus 16:20). It was designed to teach Israel one thing: daily dependence on God. You cannot stockpile God’s provision. You must return to Him every morning. But then God gave a remarkable command. Moses was told to take one jar of manna, place it before Yahweh, and preserve it as a testimony for all generations (Exodus 16:32–34). That jar of manna, which would have decayed in any ordinary container, was kept in a golden jar inside the Ark; and it did not rot.

Jesus drew this picture out into full light in John 6. He said, “Your fathers ate the manna in the wilderness, and they died. I am the living bread that came down from heaven. If anyone eats of this bread, he will live forever” (John 6:49-51). The manna in the wilderness was a shadow. Christ is the substance.
The manna fed the body for one day and then it was gone. Christ feeds the life for eternity. The manna that rotted in every ordinary vessel was preserved in the presence of God, inside the Ark; just as Christ saw no corruption in the tomb. Psalm 16:10 says, “You will not allow Your Holy One to see corruption,” and Peter quotes this directly of the resurrection in Acts 2:27. The manna that could not decay in God’s presence is a quiet prophecy of Christ that death could not hold and decay could not touch.

There is one more beautiful detail. The manna inside the Ark was hidden. The Kohathites who carried the Ark on their shoulders every day through the wilderness never saw it. They carried it without seeing it. Revelation 2:17 gives a promise to the overcomer: “I will give him some of the hidden manna.” The depths of Christ; the fullness of who He is, the nourishment that goes beyond what we have yet experienced, are hidden inside the Ark we carry. We have tasted, but there is more. The manna that was preserved in the presence of God also points to the immortal, resurrection life of Christ after He rose. He did not simply come back to the same mortal life. He entered a new and undying existence. The bread that could not spoil in the Ark speaks of the life that cannot end; the eternal life that He now shares with all who believe in Him.

title: Prayer
text: Holy Father, You placed the Ark first because You are always first. Forgive us for the times we have carried other things ahead of Your presence; our plans, our programmes, our own ideas of what the journey should look like. Teach us to put You first in the order of everything we do. Thank You that inside the Ark was everything we could never provide for ourselves, a Law we could not keep, a life we could not produce, a bread we could not bake. You have given us Christ, who fulfilled the Law on our behalf, who rose from death when we had no power to rise, and who feeds us with Himself when everything else leaves us empty. Help us to carry Him faithfully on our shoulders today; into our homes, our workplaces, our neighbourhoods, and every wilderness place we walk through. May the presence of Christ move wherever we move. And may we never grow so familiar with what we carry that we forget how holy it is. In Christ’s name, amen.
